intro.paragraph1Title: The Start of My Life in Australia
intro.paragraph1:
In 2014, I landed in Sydney with two suitcases and not a single person I knew. The share house I'd arranged from Korea turned out to be nothing like I'd pictured. After a quick hello to my new roommate, I unpacked into an empty room, then headed back down the stairs to the shopping centre nearby.

That's when it hit me — the loneliness.

"Okay. Be brave. Just make dinner. I'll feel better once I eat something."
"Focus on what you can control. School starts tomorrow — get some food ready tonight."
intro.paragraph2Title: A $30 Pot, a Bowl of Doenjang-jjigae brings me home
intro.paragraph2:
On a tight budget, the first thing I thought to cook was doenjang-jjigae. I picked up a pot and a few basics — zucchini, tofu, spring onion, chili, doenjang paste. With every vegetable I chopped, some of the tension melted away. And as the ingredients went into the pot one by one, it started looking like an actual meal. I felt lighter.

When the stew began to bubble in that unfamiliar kitchen, the smell brought me straight back to my mum's table — that same doenjang-jjigae, that same feeling of home. With every spoonful, the strangeness of my first night in Sydney softened, just a little.
intro.paragraph3Title: One Pot, Two Pots, Three Pots
intro.paragraph3:
That single pot is where Jeena's Kitchen really begins.

Cooking for myself, with just one pot, in a country I barely knew — that was its own small act of courage. Some days, figuring out what to eat and making it myself felt lonely, even exhausting. But every time I sat down to a meal I'd made, I felt better. Stronger. I wanted my whole life in Australia to feel that way.

A pot, to me, is about connection. The one I once used only for myself slowly became the one I cooked for partners and friends with. As I moved between schools and share houses, one pot turned into two, then more — and cooking became my way of connecting with people from completely different cultures and languages. It gave me something to hold onto in an unfamiliar place.
intro.paragraph4: Even on the worst days, just touching the vegetables, smelling them, taking the time to cook properly — it felt like taking care of myself. And sharing that food with a friend or a partner is when I'd really feel connected to them. Those relationships are what made my life here feel full. Let's eat a home-cooked meal together, and be grateful for it. The things Jeena's Kitchen loves are simple, everyday things that matter more than they seem to. Saying hello to a neighbour. Cooking a meal at home. Sharing food and feeling thankful for it. It might sound ordinary — but it's where I feel most at peace, and most happy.
